summary:
The Battle of Legnica (April 9, 1241 AD), fought two days before the [[event:2975|Battle of Mohi]], was the northern prong of the coordinated [[event:2227|Mongol invasion of Europe]], in which a Mongol force under Orda Khan and Baidar defeated a combined Polish and German Christian army at Legnica (Liegnitz) in modern southwestern Poland, killing Duke Henry II ('the Pious') of Silesia and eliminating the most formidable Christian military force in central Europe. The battle is famous for the alleged use of a smoke or gas weapon by the Mongols to disrupt the Christian formation — if true, one of the earliest recorded uses of chemical weapons in European warfare.

The Mongol force at Legnica was likely not the main Mongol army (which was operating in Hungary under Batu Khan and Subutai) but a screening and raiding force whose purpose was to prevent Polish and German forces from reinforcing the Hungarian theater. In this objective it succeeded perfectly: Henry II had assembled an army from Polish duchies and Teutonic Knights and was marching to join with King Béla IV of Hungary when the Mongols intercepted him. The Christian defeat at Legnica and simultaneous destruction of the Hungarian army at Mohi left no viable military force between the Mongols and the Atlantic coast of Europe. The subsequent Mongol withdrawal removed the threat, but the experience permanently altered European military thinking and investment: the decades following 1241 saw a dramatic acceleration of stone castle-building across Poland, Hungary, and Bohemia as rulers sought permanent military infrastructure that could withstand the kind of mobile cavalry assault no field army had been able to resist.
